passages.title: The Great Lakes: A Shared Treasure
passages.body:
The Great Lakes — Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario — hold about one-fifth of the world's fresh surface water. They form a natural border between Canada and the United States and support millions of people who rely on them for drinking water, transportation, fishing, and recreation.

Long before European settlers arrived, Indigenous nations lived along these shores and travelled them by canoe. Trade routes connected communities hundreds of kilometres apart. Today, huge cargo ships still carry grain, iron ore, and other goods through the lakes to ports around the world.

Keeping the lakes healthy is a major challenge. Runoff from farms and cities can carry pollutants into the water. Invasive species, such as zebra mussels, have upset food webs by filtering out plankton that native fish need. Climate change is also altering water levels and ice cover, which affects wetlands and wildlife.

Governments on both sides of the border work together through agreements to monitor water quality and limit pollution. Schools and community groups often organize shoreline cleanups. Scientists say that protecting the Great Lakes requires both large policy decisions and everyday choices, such as reducing plastic waste and being careful about what goes down storm drains.

passages.title: How a Bill Becomes a Law in Ontario
passages.body:
Ontario's laws are made by the Legislative Assembly at Queen's Park in Toronto. Most new laws begin as ideas that a government minister believes are needed. These ideas are drafted into a bill — a proposed law written in formal language.

A bill must pass through several stages. First, it is introduced and read aloud so members of the assembly know what it is about. Then committees study the bill, hear from experts and the public, and may suggest changes. After debate and votes in the assembly, a bill that passes its final vote receives Royal Assent from the Lieutenant Governor and becomes law.

Not every idea becomes a law quickly. Some bills are debated for months because members disagree about details or costs. Opposition parties may argue that a bill should be changed or rejected. In a democracy, this disagreement is expected; it is part of how citizens' different views are represented.

Once a law is in place, government departments and agencies put it into practice. Courts may later interpret the law if people disagree about what it means in a specific case.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, approximately what fraction of the world's fresh surface water do the Great Lakes hold?
choices: One-tenth
One-fifth
One-quarter
One-third
questions.answer: One-fifth

questions.prompt: Why does the passage mention canoes?
choices: To explain that the lakes are too shallow for large ships
To show that Indigenous nations used the lakes for travel and trade long ago
To argue that modern ships are worse for the environment
To describe a popular tourist activity today
questions.answer: To show that Indigenous nations used the lakes for travel and trade long ago

questions.prompt: What problem do zebra mussels cause, based on the passage?
choices: They increase the amount of plankton in the water
They filter out plankton that native fish need
They are illegal to transport through the locks
They make drinking water taste salty
questions.answer: They filter out plankton that native fish need

questions.prompt: The word "runoff" in the passage most likely refers to —
choices: water that flows over land and can carry pollutants into the lakes
ships that run off course during storms
fish that swim away from invasive species
ice that runs off the surface in spring
questions.answer: water that flows over land and can carry pollutants into the lakes
questions.hintContext: Read the sentence about farms and cities — what is moving into the water?

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: The Great Lakes are only important for shipping grain
The Great Lakes are a vital resource that faces serious threats and needs many kinds of protection
Zebra mussels are the only danger to the Great Lakes
Canada and the United States should stop sharing the Great Lakes
questions.answer: The Great Lakes are a vital resource that faces serious threats and needs many kinds of protection

questions.prompt: According to the passage, climate change affects the Great Lakes by —
choices: making the water completely fresh
increasing the number of cargo ships
changing water levels and ice cover
removing all invasive species
questions.answer: changing water levels and ice cover

questions.prompt: Why does the author include the detail about shoreline cleanups?
choices: To show that only scientists can protect the lakes
To give an example of how ordinary people can help protect the lakes
To prove that cleanups are more important than laws
To explain how bills become laws
questions.answer: To give an example of how ordinary people can help protect the lakes

questions.prompt: Which detail from the passage is a fact rather than an opinion?
choices: Protecting the lakes is easy
The Great Lakes support millions of people
Cargo ships are ugly
Everyone should move away from the lakes
questions.answer: The Great Lakes support millions of people
questions.hintContext: A fact can be checked; an opinion is what someone thinks or feels.

questions.prompt: Where does Ontario's Legislative Assembly meet?
choices: Ottawa
Queen's Park in Toronto
Windsor
Thunder Bay
questions.answer: Queen's Park in Toronto

questions.prompt: What is a bill, according to the passage?
choices: A speech given by the Lieutenant Governor
A proposed law written in formal language
A committee of experts
A type of court decision
questions.answer: A proposed law written in formal language

questions.prompt: What happens after a bill passes its final vote in the assembly?
choices: It is automatically sent to the United Nations
It receives Royal Assent from the Lieutenant Governor and becomes law
It must be approved by every city mayor
It is stored until the next election
questions.answer: It receives Royal Assent from the Lieutenant Governor and becomes law

questions.prompt: Why does the passage say that disagreement among parties is "expected" in a democracy?
choices: Because laws should never change
Because different views of citizens are represented and debated
Because committees always make mistakes
Because bills are never read aloud
questions.answer: Because different views of citizens are represented and debated

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who may suggest changes to a bill?
choices: Only the Lieutenant Governor
Committees, after hearing from experts and the public
Only students in Grade 5
Only cargo ships on the Great Lakes
questions.answer: Committees, after hearing from experts and the public

questions.prompt: What role do courts play, based on the last paragraph?
choices: They write new bills every week
They may interpret a law when people disagree about its meaning
They replace the Legislative Assembly
They organize public cleanups
questions.answer: They may interpret a law when people disagree about its meaning

questions.prompt: Which title would best fit the second passage?
choices: The History of Canoes on the Great Lakes
The Path from Idea to Law in Ontario
How Zebra Mussels Invaded North America
Why Climate Change Is Fake
questions.answer: The Path from Idea to Law in Ontario

questions.prompt: A classmate says: "If one invasive species hurts the Great Lakes, then every new species that enters the lakes must be harmful." What is the main flaw in this reasoning?
choices: It confuses fresh water with salt water
It assumes all new species are harmful because one example is harmful
It ignores the role of the Lieutenant Governor
It uses too many statistics
questions.answer: It assumes all new species are harmful because one example is harmful
questions.hintContext: Can you conclude something about ALL species from ONE bad example?

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains only a fact?
choices: Queen's Park is the most boring building in Canada
Royal Assent is given by the Lieutenant Governor
Everyone agrees that laws should pass in one day
Committees are always useless
questions.answer: Royal Assent is given by the Lieutenant Governor

questions.prompt: If a new law banned single-use plastic bottles near Great Lakes beaches, which group from the passages would put that law into daily practice?
choices: The Legislative Assembly only, with no one else involved
Government departments and agencies
Zebra mussels
Cargo ship captains only
questions.answer: Government departments and agencies
questions.hintContext: Re-read the last sentence of the bill passage about who carries out laws.

questions.prompt: The phrase "put it into practice" in the bill passage is closest in meaning to —
choices: argue about it in committee
carry it out in real life
delete it from the books
read it aloud once
questions.answer: carry it out in real life

questions.prompt: Both passages emphasize that important problems — lake health and lawmaking — require —
choices: ignoring other people's opinions
only one quick solution
cooperation, time, and more than one kind of effort
getting rid of all ships
questions.answer: cooperation, time, and more than one kind of effort
questions.hintContext: Think about cross-border work and committees, debate, and many actors in each passage.
